summary: Creates an ongoing conversation between King’s and every student about their purpose, ambitions, needs and trajectory, helping them take greater ownership of their journey and navigate the opportunities through which they can learn, connect and contribute.
detailSummary:
Beginning when a student accepts their offer and continuing throughout their time at King’s, this deliverable establishes a regular, supportive rhythm of reflection and conversation. Students are invited to explore how they are feeling, what matters to them, what they hope to achieve and contribute, where they need connection or support, and how their direction may be changing. Uncertainty is treated as a legitimate starting point, and purpose is understood broadly across learning, personal development, belonging, service, contribution, experience and future direction.

At its centre is the King’s Canvas, a living and self-curated picture of the experience each student wants to build. Students can revisit and reshape their Canvas over time, using it to connect their academic learning with co-curricular and extra-curricular opportunities, relationships, communities and future ambitions. Digital tools and carefully bounded uses of AI provide regular prompts, help students make connections and surface relevant opportunities at scale. This technological enablement docks into existing human relationships at meaningful moments, allowing personal tutors, careers advisers and others to add context, judgement and care without requiring a new layer of continuous one-to-one provision.

King’s also becomes more responsive to what students express. Insight into their ambitions and needs helps the institution connect students with relevant opportunities and make better-informed decisions about what to commission, expand, target or adapt. This creates a more coherent relationship between individual purpose and the wider ecology of opportunity at King’s, while increasing confidence in the reach, relevance and return on investment of enrichment provision.
problemSolved: Universities tend to organise and understand student journeys through courses and institutional processes, leaving individual purpose and the broader developmental value of university implicit. At King’s, a rich but fragmented opportunity ecology is difficult to navigate and rewards students who already possess confidence, networks and institutional knowledge.
whatChanges: King’s establishes an ongoing, technologically enabled conversation that helps every student explore and revise their purpose, shape an individual trajectory through the university, connect with relevant opportunities and contribute insight that makes enrichment provision more responsive.

caseForChange.problem:
Universities have long promised more than disciplinary knowledge. They are presented as places where students discover who they are, develop their interests and direction, form relationships and communities, and acquire broad capabilities as thinkers, collaborators, creators and citizens.

Much of this wider value remains implicit and underassured. It is often treated as something students absorb through exposure to university life rather than something the institution intentionally helps every student to recognise, shape and develop.

The dominant model of the student journey compounds this problem. Universities tend to map journeys through courses and institutional processes: enrolment, modules, assessment, progression and graduation. The course provides an essential academic spine, but it cannot fully represent the trajectory held by the individual student. That trajectory may connect closely to their discipline, move across several fields or lead towards a future only tangentially related to their course.

King’s therefore has limited means of making each student’s wider journey visible. Its rich ecology of academic, co-curricular and extra-curricular opportunities sits around the course but is often fragmented and difficult to navigate. Students are left to work out for themselves what matters, how different experiences connect and what they may be missing. Those with greater confidence, networks or familiarity with university culture are better placed to do this, reinforcing inequalities in who can turn the breadth of university into meaningful personal value.
caseForChange.opportunity:
King’s can make individual student purpose a visible and supported part of the university experience. An ongoing conversation can help each student explore what they want from King’s, recognise how that purpose changes and connect their academic journey with ambitions for personal development, belonging, contribution, experience and future direction.

Purpose in this context extends beyond personal fulfilment or career planning. It includes how students understand their relationship with the world beyond themselves, the communities to which they belong, the responsibilities they may choose to take on and the contribution their knowledge and capabilities could enable them to make.

This gives King’s a way to understand the student journey as an individual trajectory rather than only a route through a course. The course remains central, while the student gains a coherent way to connect it with the wider experiences, relationships and opportunities through which university creates value.

The King’s Canvas gives that trajectory a living form. Technology sustains the regular rhythm, helps students connect different parts of their experience and brings relevant opportunities into view. Existing human relationships provide interpretation, challenge, encouragement and care at the moments when these matter most.

King’s can also respond to patterns in students’ expressed ambitions and needs, allowing opportunities to be recommended, commissioned and developed with greater confidence.
caseForChange.whyNow:
The sector can no longer rely on the broader benefits of university remaining implicit. Students increasingly bear the cost of their education directly, with international students making a particularly significant investment. Economic, technological and cultural change is disrupting established graduate pathways and intensifying scrutiny of the graduate premium.

Students need to see how their education, experiences and development combine into something personally meaningful and valuable. Institutions need to demonstrate how they support that process intentionally and equitably.

For King’s, this is a contemporary expression of knowledge with purpose and its tradition of service. It makes the development of purpose an active part of the educational relationship, helping students consider both what they want from their university experience and what their knowledge, capabilities and relationships might enable them to contribute to society.
caseForChange.intendedChange:
King’s establishes an ongoing institutional conversation with every student, beginning at offer acceptance and continuing throughout their university journey. Students are supported to explore and revise their purposes, connect their course with the wider university experience and make intentional choices about the knowledge, relationships, communities, capabilities and opportunities they want to develop.

The individual student journey becomes visible as something held and shaped by the student, supported by the institution and responsive to change. Technology enables this conversation to operate at scale, while existing human relationships provide support and judgement at meaningful points.

King’s connects students with relevant opportunities and uses their expressed ambitions and needs to shape future provision. The broader developmental value of university becomes more intentional, more equitable and more clearly experienced by students.
